title: Practice Exam #3 (Cloud Database Engineer)
description: Google Cloud Certified Professional Cloud Database Engineer - 50 questions

questions.text: A SaaS company operates a Cloud SQL for MySQL instance that serves as the backend database for its customer-facing application. The workload follows a consistent weekly pattern with heavy usage on weekdays and minimal activity on weekends. The operations team initially provisioned the instance with a large machine type to ensure stability during peak demand, but recent cost reviews reveal that the instance is significantly oversized relative to actual resource consumption. You want to lower the monthly Cloud SQL bill while maintaining reliable performance during periods of high activity. What should you do?

options.text: Set up a Cloud Scheduler job that invokes a Cloud Function to shut down the instance on Friday evening and restart it on Monday morning
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to leverage the Cloud SQL overprovisioned instance recommender, which examines historical resource utilization data and generates specific right-sizing suggestions tailored to your actual workload. Rather than guessing which machine type might be appropriate, this recommender provides evidence-based guidance that accounts for peak usage periods. By applying its recommendations, you can confidently downsize the instance to a tier that still satisfies your heaviest workload demands while eliminating the cost of unused CPU and memory. This is the safest and most precise method to reduce spending without risking performance degradation.
Shutting down the instance on weekends with a scheduler saves some cost during idle periods but does nothing about the fact that the machine type is too large during the five days it is actually running, which is where most of the expense accumulates. It also risks data unavailability if any weekend batch jobs or maintenance processes need database access. Enabling automatic storage increase only manages disk capacity and has no effect on vCPU or memory costs, which are the primary drivers of overspending in this scenario. Switching to a smaller memory tier without data-driven analysis could lead to memory pressure and performance issues under peak load. Adding a cross-region read replica introduces additional network latency and doubles infrastructure costs by running a second instance, which contradicts the goal of reducing expenses.

questions.text: A logistics company runs a Cloud SQL for MySQL instance that powers its order management system. During the upcoming holiday season, the company anticipates a significant spike in new order insertions and inventory updates across all warehouses simultaneously. The database team has observed that CPU and memory utilization on the primary instance are already consistently above 80% during normal operations. The company needs to ensure the database can handle the expected write-heavy workload while keeping transaction latency low, and they must continue using Cloud SQL. What should the database engineer do to prepare the instance?

options.text: Increase the instance machine type by adding more vCPUs and memory
options.explanation:
Vertically scaling the primary Cloud SQL instance is the right approach when write-heavy workloads are pushing the existing hardware to its limits. In a relational database like MySQL, all write operations must go through the primary node to preserve transactional consistency. By upgrading to a larger machine type with additional vCPUs, the database engine can process more concurrent connections and parallel operations. More memory means a larger buffer pool, which reduces the frequency of expensive disk reads and allows the engine to keep more working data in memory. This is a straightforward way to increase throughput on the primary instance without requiring application changes or architectural redesigns.
Read replicas are useful when the bottleneck is caused by SELECT queries competing with writes, but they cannot accept write traffic themselves, so they would not help absorb the surge of new orders and inventory updates. A high availability configuration with automatic failover provides resilience against outages and zone-level failures, but the standby replica does not serve traffic and does not add any processing capacity to the primary node. Configuring an external replica through binary log replication is typically used to feed data into systems outside of Cloud SQL, such as analytics environments or migration targets, and actually adds replication overhead to the primary instance rather than relieving it. None of these three alternatives address the fundamental problem of insufficient CPU and memory on the instance that must handle all the incoming writes.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company stores patient monitoring data and clinical research datasets in a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database with 16 vCPUs and 64 GB of RAM. Research analysts run complex aggregation queries across tables containing hundreds of millions of patient vitals records to identify treatment outcome patterns, which regularly pushes CPU utilization above 85% and causes latency spikes on the electronic health record (EHR) application that clinicians depend on. The database receives a constant stream of concurrent writes from thousands of connected medical devices while also serving heavy read traffic from clinical dashboards. With plans to onboard several new hospital networks and a requirement that research workloads never degrade the responsiveness of the live patient monitoring system, you need an architecture that offers elastic horizontal read scaling and purpose-built acceleration of analytical query processing. What should you implement?

options.text: Transition to a Cloud SQL High Availability configuration with 32 vCPUs and integrated PgBouncer connection pooling
options.explanation:
AlloyDB is the right solution here because it was built to handle hybrid transactional and analytical processing (HTAP) workloads where real-time writes and heavy analytical queries must coexist without interfering with each other. The columnar engine maintains a memory-resident columnar data format that dramatically speeds up the large-scale aggregations and scans the research analysts need to run across hundreds of millions of rows. Read pools allow analytical query traffic to be directed to horizontally scalable, independent compute resources that do not consume capacity on the primary instance, which means the live patient monitoring writes and clinical dashboard reads remain unaffected. This separation of concerns combined with elastic scaling makes it the ideal architecture as new hospital networks are onboarded.
Doubling the vCPU count on a standard Cloud SQL instance with High Availability and adding PgBouncer addresses connection management and failover but does nothing to isolate analytical workloads from transactional ones, and the CPU contention problem would return as data volumes grow. Moving to an Enterprise Plus Cloud SQL instance with read replicas does help distribute read traffic, but those replicas process queries using the same row-oriented storage engine and lack the specialized columnar processing needed to efficiently handle complex analytical scans over massive datasets. Keeping the existing Cloud SQL instance and adding materialized views only helps with a fixed set of pre-computed queries and does not provide the workload isolation or elastic scaling the company needs as it expands. Increasing SSD storage addresses disk capacity but has no meaningful impact on the CPU saturation caused by analytical query processing.

questions.text: A healthcare technology company runs a PostgreSQL database on-premises that stores patient appointment scheduling data. They have initiated a continuous migration to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL using the Database Migration Service. The migration job has already completed the initial full load and is now in the change data capture (CDC) phase, streaming ongoing changes to the destination. A developer has just added a new table called `appointment_reminders` to the source database to support a new feature. The team notices that this table is not appearing in the Cloud SQL destination. What should you do to ensure the new table is included in the active replication stream?

options.text: Run pglogical.replication_set_add_table on the source database to register the new table with the default replication set
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to use the pglogical extension on the source database to add the newly created table to the replication set. Database Migration Service relies on the pglogical extension to handle continuous replication from PostgreSQL sources. When a migration job is already running, it only replicates tables that were registered in the replication set at the time the job was configured. New tables created after the migration started are not automatically detected or added to the replication stream. By running pglogical.replication_set_add_table, you explicitly register the table with the active replication set, which allows the logical decoding process to pick it up and begin streaming its changes to the destination without interrupting the existing migration.
Stopping and restarting the entire migration job would require repeating the full initial dump of all tables, which wastes significant time and resources when only a single new table needs to be added. The Google Cloud Console does not expose an option to dynamically add individual tables to a migration job that is already in the CDC phase. Manually exporting and importing the table using pg_dump and restore would place the table outside of the active replication pipeline, meaning future inserts, updates, and deletes to that table would not be captured, leading to data inconsistency between the source and destination over time.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics platform runs on Google Cloud and relies on a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance to store patient appointment records. Every Monday morning, a reporting dashboard used by hundreds of clinic managers generates thousands of concurrent read queries against the database. This weekly spike causes severe slowdowns in the appointment booking system, which depends on the same database instance to process new reservations and schedule updates. The engineering team needs to alleviate the read congestion without disrupting the write path used by the booking system. What should you do?

options.text: Enable high availability on the Cloud SQL instance with a standby replica in a separate zone within the same region
options.explanation:
The right approach here is to introduce a read replica that absorbs the heavy reporting workload away from the primary instance. Cloud SQL read replicas serve read-only traffic on a separate compute resource, which means the primary instance can continue handling writes such as new appointment bookings and schedule changes without being impacted by the dashboard queries. By directing the reporting dashboard specifically to the replica endpoint, you split the workload across two instances in a way that matches the natural read-write division of the application. This is the standard architectural pattern in relational databases when read-heavy traffic creates contention with transactional write operations.
Deploying the Auth Proxy improves connection security and simplifies authentication, but it does not add compute capacity or distribute query load across multiple instances, so it would not resolve the bottleneck caused by thousands of concurrent reads. Increasing the machine type is a vertical scaling approach that can offer short-term relief, but it requires a restart of the instance, introduces a cost ceiling, and does not architecturally separate the two competing workloads. Enabling high availability creates a standby replica that exists solely to take over if the primary fails, but the standby does not serve any traffic during normal operations and therefore provides no relief from read congestion.

options.explanation:
The best approach here is to combine Cloud Scheduler with a Cloud Function to handle the nightly export. Cloud Scheduler serves as a fully managed cron replacement that can trigger events on a defined schedule without any server to maintain. The Cloud Function contains the export logic and runs only when invoked, meaning you pay nothing when the function is idle between nightly runs. This serverless combination eliminates the need to patch, monitor, or scale any underlying infrastructure. It directly addresses both goals of low operational overhead and minimal cost.
A Dataflow streaming pipeline is designed to handle continuous or complex data processing workloads and would be far more expensive and complicated than what a simple nightly export requires. Cloud Composer is built on Apache Airflow and is well suited to orchestrating multi-step workflows with complex dependencies, but it carries a significant baseline monthly cost and management burden that is unjustified when you only need to run one straightforward task per night. questions.text: A healthcare analytics startup stores millions of clinical research paper abstracts in a Cloud SQL for MySQL database. Researchers want to discover papers that are conceptually related to a natural language description they provide, such as "treatments that reduce inflammation through gut microbiome modulation," even when the abstracts use completely different terminology. How should you design this search functionality?

options.text: Generate and store vector embeddings of the abstracts and perform a nearest neighbor search to identify the most similar results
options.explanation:
The correct approach here is to convert the research abstracts into vector embeddings and then use nearest neighbor search to match against the researcher's query. When text is transformed into high-dimensional numerical vectors using an embedding model, the system captures the semantic meaning of the content rather than relying on specific words. This means a researcher can describe a concept in their own words and the system will return abstracts that discuss the same idea, even if the vocabulary is entirely different. Cloud SQL for MySQL supports storing these vectors and performing neighbor searches to find the closest matches based on mathematical distance between vectors.
Full-text search with MATCH AGAINST operates on keyword matching and term frequency, which means it would miss abstracts that discuss the same concept using different words or phrasing. A researcher looking for "gut microbiome modulation" would not find a paper that describes "intestinal flora intervention" unless those exact terms appeared. Computing Levenshtein distance measures how many single-character edits are needed to transform one string into another, which is useful for catching typos or slight misspellings but has no ability to understand that two different words share a meaning. Using JSON_EXTRACT with a trigger assumes that someone has already manually categorized every abstract with structured metadata tags, which defeats the purpose of allowing freeform natural language queries and does not scale when dealing with millions of documents that lack such tagging.

questions.text: A financial services company runs a transaction monitoring platform that stores real-time event data in Cloud Bigtable. After a recent acquisition, the platform began ingesting data from the acquired company's systems, which introduced a large volume of new accounts and different access patterns. Since the integration, the team has observed hotspotting on specific nodes and inconsistent response times across different parts of the application. The team needs to resolve these issues while keeping the architecture straightforward. What should you do? (Select two)

options.text: Switch all application traffic to multi-cluster routing across every available region
options.explanation:
The best approach here involves addressing both the data layout and the request routing to resolve the hotspotting and latency inconsistencies. When new access patterns are introduced, such as after an acquisition, the existing row key design may no longer distribute data evenly, causing certain nodes to become overloaded while others remain underutilized. Revisiting the row key schema and column family structure helps spread reads and writes more uniformly across all nodes in the cluster. Additionally, configuring app profiles with single-cluster routing ensures that the newly integrated workload is directed consistently to one cluster, which reduces latency variability that can occur when requests are unpredictably routed to distant clusters. Together, these changes target the root causes of the observed performance degradation without adding significant operational overhead.
Archiving older records to Cloud Storage would reduce storage volume but would not address the real-time access pattern issues causing hotspots on active data. Reducing the number of Bigtable nodes removes available capacity and increases the likelihood of resource contention, which would make performance worse rather than better. Enabling multi-cluster routing across all regions can cause requests to be served by geographically distant clusters, which introduces higher latency and potential consistency challenges, especially when the workload benefits from predictable and localized routing. None of these alternatives address the fundamental problems of uneven data distribution or inconsistent request routing that are driving the performance issues.

questions.text: A financial services company maintains a Firestore database that tracks customer account activity throughout the day. Compliance regulations require that a complete copy of the database be exported to a Cloud Storage bucket every night at midnight. The engineering team wants to minimize operational overhead and avoid paying for idle resources. What is the most cost-effective and fully managed way to automate this recurring export?

options.text: Deploy Cloud Scheduler to invoke a Cloud Function that triggers the database export
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to combine Cloud Scheduler with a Cloud Function to handle the nightly Firestore export. Cloud Scheduler serves as a fully managed cron service that can fire on a precise schedule, and the Cloud Function provides just enough compute to call the Firestore export API. Because the function only runs during the brief export invocation, you are billed for seconds of execution rather than hours of idle infrastructure. This combination requires no servers to patch, no containers to build, and supports automatic retries if something goes wrong. It is the standard serverless pattern recommended by Google Cloud when you need to trigger a simple operation on a recurring schedule.
Using a Compute Engine VM means provisioning and maintaining an operating system, and the instance would sit idle for nearly the entire day while still incurring costs. An App Engine cron job can technically schedule tasks, but it introduces unnecessary complexity compared to a lightweight function when the only requirement is a single API call. A Cloud Run service would work functionally, but it adds the overhead of building and managing a container image, which is overkill when the task is this straightforward. Any approach that depends on persistent infrastructure increases both cost and the risk of failure if the underlying instance becomes unavailable.

questions.text: A logistics company maintains its shipment tracking records in a Cloud SQL for MySQL database that is continuously updated as packages move through the delivery network. The analytics team has built a fraud detection model that needs to score incoming shipments in near real-time by cross-referencing live tracking updates with years of historical shipping patterns stored in BigQuery. Leadership has emphasized that the solution must reduce unnecessary data duplication and eliminate delays caused by batch data transfers between systems. What should you do?

options.text: Set up a Datastream job to continuously replicate Cloud SQL updates into BigQuery and run the BigQuery ML model against the replicated data
options.explanation:
The best solution is to use BigQuery federated queries to reach into the Cloud SQL instance and combine that live data with the historical records already in BigQuery. A federated query allows BigQuery to treat the external MySQL database as if it were a native table, pulling the latest shipment tracking records on demand without physically copying them into a separate location. This means the fraud detection model running in BigQuery ML always has access to the freshest data and can score incoming shipments with minimal delay. Because no intermediate storage or transformation pipeline is required, this approach directly addresses the constraint around reducing data duplication and avoiding batch transfer latency.
Exporting years of historical data from BigQuery into a Cloud SQL instance is impractical because relational databases are not designed to handle warehouse-scale analytical workloads and machine learning computations efficiently. Modifying the application to perform dual writes into both Cloud SQL and BigQuery adds significant complexity to the application layer and creates risks around data consistency if one write succeeds and the other fails. While Datastream is a capable change data capture tool that could keep BigQuery synchronized with Cloud SQL, it still involves creating and maintaining a second copy of the data, which conflicts with the stated goal of minimizing duplication. It also introduces some replication lag that would not meet the near real-time requirement as cleanly as a federated query. Using BigQuery as the central computation engine with a federated connection to the transactional database is the most streamlined architecture that satisfies all of the stated constraints.

options.explanation:
The best solution is to use BigQuery federated queries to reach into the Cloud SQL instance and combine that live data with the historical records already in BigQuery. A federated query allows BigQuery to treat the external MySQL database as if it were a native table, pulling the latest shipment tracking records on demand without physically copying them into a separate location. This means the fraud detection model running in BigQuery ML always has access to the freshest data and can score incoming shipments with minimal delay. Because no intermediate storage or transformation pipeline is required, this approach directly addresses the constraint around reducing data duplication and avoiding batch transfer latency.
Exporting years of historical data from BigQuery into a Cloud SQL instance is impractical because relational databases are not designed to handle warehouse-scale analytical workloads and machine learning computations efficiently. Modifying the application to perform dual writes into both Cloud SQL and BigQuery adds significant complexity to the application layer and creates risks around data consistency if one write succeeds and the other fails. While Datastream is a capable change data capture tool that could keep BigQuery synchronized with Cloud SQL, it still involves creating and maintaining a second copy of the data, which conflicts with the stated goal of minimizing duplication. It also introduces some replication lag that would not meet the near real-time requirement as cleanly as a federated query. Using BigQuery as the central computation engine with a federated connection to the transactional database is the most streamlined architecture that satisfies all of the stated constraints.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company is building a HIPAA-compliant data processing pipeline on Google Cloud. The pipeline uses GKE-based microservices and Compute Engine batch processing jobs that must connect to a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database containing sensitive patient records. A distributed team of database administrators needs direct access to the database from various locations to perform maintenance and troubleshooting. The organization wants to ensure that every database connection can be traced back to a specific identity and that no long-lived credentials are stored anywhere in the system. What is the best way to configure database access in this environment?

options.text: Establish VPC Service Controls around the Cloud SQL instances and distribute unique static database certificates to each authorized administrator and service
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to use IAM database authentication combined with the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy, assigning Cloud SQL IAM roles to service accounts that represent GKE and Compute Engine workloads while requiring human administrators to authenticate with their own IAM identities. This configuration leverages Workload Identity in GKE so that pods authenticate as IAM service accounts without needing stored credentials, and Compute Engine workloads can use their attached service accounts in the same way. The Cloud SQL Auth Proxy handles TLS encryption automatically and validates IAM credentials on every connection attempt, which means every session is tied to a traceable identity. This satisfies the requirement that no long-lived passwords exist in the system and that all access is individually auditable, which is critical in a HIPAA-regulated environment.
Using Secret Manager to distribute administrative passwords still relies on database-native credentials that must be rotated manually, and storing even encrypted passwords creates risk when the goal is to eliminate static credentials entirely. Wrapping instances with VPC Service Controls and distributing static database certificates provides network perimeter protection but does not address identity-level authentication at the database layer, and managing individual certificates across a distributed team introduces significant operational burden. A shared bastion host with a single superuser account completely undermines the traceability requirement because all administrator activity would appear under one identity, making it impossible to audit who performed which action. Firewall restrictions on a bastion host add network security but do nothing to distinguish between individual users once they reach the database.

questions.text: A logistics company stores shipment tracking data in Cloud Bigtable, ingesting millions of scan events per day from distribution centers worldwide. The customer-facing tracking portal works without issues, but the operations team notices that queries powering a particular analytics report for the Asia-Pacific region consistently time out every morning between 9:00 AM and 10:00 AM local time. The rest of the day, the same report runs within acceptable limits. What is the best first step to determine the underlying cause of this recurring performance degradation?

options.text: Adjust the garbage collection policy on the affected table
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to use the Key Visualizer tool to examine heatmap data and understand access patterns during the problematic window. Since the slowdown is confined to a specific time range and affects only one particular workload, the most likely explanation is that read or write traffic is concentrating on a narrow range of row keys. The Key Visualizer heatmaps show exactly which row key ranges are experiencing elevated pressure, making it possible to identify hot-spotting on specific tablets. If the Asia-Pacific shipment data shares a common row key prefix, all of those scan events could be landing on the same tablet and overwhelming it during peak morning ingestion. This diagnostic visibility is essential before making any architectural changes, because it confirms whether the row key design needs to be restructured to distribute load more evenly across nodes.
Adjusting garbage collection policies controls how old cell versions are cleaned up and has no bearing on why active queries are timing out during a specific hour. Provisioning a separate instance adds operational complexity and cost without addressing the root cause, which could follow the data into the new instance if the schema is the problem. Reviewing total storage consumption tells you how much disk space is being used but provides no insight into traffic distribution or which row keys are creating bottlenecks. None of these alternatives give you the granular, time-based visibility needed to diagnose a pattern that is both time-bound and workload-specific.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics firm needs to replicate data from its on-premises PostgreSQL patient scheduling system into BigQuery so that operational leaders can monitor appointment trends and resource utilization with minimal delay. The team wants a fully managed approach that avoids building custom extraction logic and delivers data to BigQuery as close to real time as possible. Which solution best meets these requirements?

options.text: Use Database Migration Service to migrate the PostgreSQL database into Cloud SQL and then access the data through BigQuery federated queries
options.explanation:
The correct approach here is to use Datastream combined with Dataflow to create a managed CDC pipeline from the on-premises PostgreSQL database into BigQuery. Datastream captures inserts, updates, and deletes from the source database and writes them to Cloud Storage in near real time. Dataflow then reads those change events and applies them to the destination BigQuery tables. This architecture is purpose-built to handle low-latency replication from operational databases into Google Cloud without requiring the team to develop or maintain custom extraction code. The entire pipeline scales automatically and handles schema changes and connectivity challenges as a managed service.
Migrating the database into Cloud SQL through Database Migration Service and then layering BigQuery federated queries on top introduces an unnecessary intermediate database and relies on external dataset queries that tend to have higher latency and lower performance than reading from native BigQuery storage. Using Cloud Data Fusion with a JDBC connector is fundamentally a batch or micro-batch pattern that polls the source on a schedule rather than streaming changes as they happen, which adds latency and does not satisfy the near-real-time requirement as effectively. Building a custom application to export Avro files on a timer and trigger load jobs is a brittle approach that requires ongoing maintenance, cannot easily handle change tracking, and is inherently batch-oriented rather than event-driven. None of these alternatives match the low-latency, fully managed replication that the Datastream and Dataflow combination provides.

questions.text: A financial services company runs a Cloud SQL for MySQL instance that processes thousands of loan application records per minute during business hours. The database team has been asked to implement a disaster recovery strategy that supports restoration to any precise moment within the past seven days without degrading write performance on the production instance. What is the most effective way to meet this requirement?

options.text: Use serverless export operations triggered every three hours and store the output files in a multi-region Cloud Storage bucket
options.explanation:
The best approach is to enable automated backups along with point-in-time recovery and retain both backups and binary logs for seven days. Cloud SQL automated backups use lightweight snapshots that do not block writes, which is critical when the production instance must remain responsive under heavy transactional load. Point-in-time recovery works by replaying binary log entries on top of the most recent backup snapshot, which means an administrator can restore the database to any specific second within the retention window. Scheduling the backup window during off-peak hours and maintaining a full week of transaction logs ensures the team can meet their recovery objective with minimal impact on the application.
On-demand backups taken every six hours would leave gaps between snapshots and cannot provide second-level recovery granularity on their own because they lack the continuous binary log chain that point-in-time recovery depends on. Exporting data from a read replica to Cloud Storage on an hourly basis offloads processing from the primary instance, but the resulting flat files only represent the database state at the time of each export, making precise restoration to an arbitrary second impractical without extensive manual reconstruction. Serverless exports every three hours share the same limitation since they produce periodic snapshots of the data rather than a continuous recovery timeline, and they are better suited to data portability tasks than to fine-grained disaster recovery scenarios.

questions.text: A nationwide logistics company is migrating its real-time shipment tracking database to a single-region Google Cloud Spanner instance. The system must handle peak traffic of 40,000 read operations per second and 11,500 write operations per second, with an average row size of 1 KB. The engineering team needs to provision the Spanner instance to meet these performance requirements while keeping costs as low as possible. How many nodes should be provisioned?

options.text: Provision a 6-node Spanner instance
options.explanation:
The correct number of nodes is six because the write workload is the limiting factor in this scenario. Each Cloud Spanner node in a single-region configuration can handle approximately 10,000 reads per second and 2,000 writes per second when rows are around 1 KB. The 40,000 read operations per second would require 4 nodes of read capacity, while the 11,500 write operations per second divided by 2,000 writes per node yields 5.75 nodes of write capacity. Rounding up to the next whole number means 6 nodes are needed to satisfy the write demand, which is the more resource-intensive dimension. This configuration provides just enough capacity to handle both peak read and write traffic without wasting money on unnecessary compute.
A 2-node instance would only support 4,000 writes per second, falling far short of the 11,500 write requirement and making it entirely unsuitable. A 4-node instance would handle the read workload adequately at 40,000 reads per second but would max out at 8,000 writes per second, leaving a significant gap during peak write periods. An 8-node instance would comfortably handle the entire workload but would leave roughly two full nodes worth of capacity sitting idle, making it a poor choice when cost efficiency is a stated priority. Provisioning exactly 6 nodes satisfies both the read and write throughput requirements with minimal overhead.

questions.text: Your team manages a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL High Availability instance that supports a healthcare application subject to strict regulatory requirements. A critical maintenance update has been made available by Google that addresses a vulnerability in the database engine's authentication module. Google's automatic maintenance is scheduled for the following Wednesday, but your security team has mandated that the patch must be applied tonight between 11 PM and 1 AM to meet an audit deadline tomorrow afternoon. How should you ensure the patch is applied across the entire instance cluster within the required timeframe?

options.text: Modify the weekly maintenance window to tonight at 11 PM so the instance picks up the pending update
options.explanation:
The right approach here is to use the self-service maintenance capability on the primary Cloud SQL instance during the approved maintenance window. When self-service maintenance is triggered on a primary instance, Cloud SQL automatically orchestrates the update across all associated components, including high availability standby instances and any read replicas. This means the administrator does not need to separately update each node. The platform handles the sequencing to maintain data integrity and keep the cluster in a consistent state. By initiating this during the 11 PM window, the team satisfies the audit deadline while relying on the managed service to coordinate the rollout efficiently.
Changing the weekly maintenance window only affects when future recurring maintenance will occur and does not cause a pending update to be applied immediately. Switching the maintenance track to the preview channel controls whether an instance receives early-access features and updates, but it is not a mechanism to force a specific pending security patch to be applied on demand. Attempting to manually trigger self-service maintenance on each standby and read replica individually is not the intended workflow in Cloud SQL and risks creating version inconsistencies across the cluster. Cloud SQL expects the update signal to originate from the primary instance, which then propagates the changes automatically to dependent nodes.

questions.text: A healthcare technology startup operates a patient management system built on PostgreSQL. The application handles a high volume of concurrent transactions including appointment scheduling, prescription processing, and insurance claim submissions. The data science team needs to run complex analytical queries against the live operational data to identify trends in patient outcomes, detect billing anomalies, and generate compliance reports, all with minimal delay. Currently, the team copies data nightly into a separate reporting database, which causes stale results and adds maintenance burden. The startup plans to move to Google Cloud and wants to eliminate the need to replicate data into a separate system while still supporting both heavy transactional processing and real-time analytical queries using a single managed database that is compatible with their existing PostgreSQL codebase. Which Google Cloud database service should the startup use?

options.text: BigQuery
options.explanation:
AlloyDB is the right choice here because it is purpose-built to handle both high-throughput transactional workloads and analytical queries on the same dataset without requiring data movement to a separate system. It maintains full PostgreSQL compatibility, which means the startup can migrate their existing application with minimal code changes. The architecture of AlloyDB includes a columnar engine that accelerates analytical queries alongside standard row-based transactional processing, allowing the data science team to query live operational data in near real time. This eliminates the nightly replication process and the maintenance overhead that comes with it. By consolidating both workload types into a single managed service, the startup simplifies its infrastructure while meeting the performance demands of both teams.
Cloud SQL with PostgreSQL is a solid managed PostgreSQL offering, but it lacks the architectural optimizations needed to efficiently run complex analytical queries alongside high-volume transactional workloads on the same instance. Cloud Spanner provides global distribution, horizontal scalability, and strong consistency, but it does not offer PostgreSQL compatibility and is not specifically architected to blend transactional and analytical processing in a unified engine. BigQuery excels at large-scale analytical processing and can handle complex queries over massive datasets, but it cannot serve as a primary transactional database capable of handling high-concurrency writes like appointment scheduling and claim submissions. None of these alternatives satisfy the combined requirement of PostgreSQL compatibility, high-performance transactions, and real-time analytics within a single database service.

questions.text: A financial services company stores transaction records in a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database. A regulatory requirement mandates that a structured export of specific transaction tables be delivered to a Coldline Storage bucket every first day of the month at 2:00 AM. The engineering team wants to build this workflow using fully managed Google Cloud services without provisioning or maintaining any virtual machines. Which architecture should you implement?

options.text: Configure a Cloud Scheduler job to publish a message to a Pub/Sub topic that invokes a Cloud Run function
options.explanation:
The correct approach here is to combine Cloud Scheduler, Pub/Sub, and a Cloud Run function into a serverless pipeline. Cloud Scheduler serves as a fully managed cron service capable of firing at a precise schedule, such as the first of every month at 2:00 AM. When it publishes a message to a Pub/Sub topic, that message triggers a Cloud Run function that contains the logic to call the Cloud SQL Admin API and initiate the export to the designated Storage bucket. This architecture is entirely serverless, meaning there are no servers to patch, no uptime to guarantee, and you only pay when the function actually executes. It provides a reliable, decoupled, and cost-effective way to meet recurring export requirements.
Scheduling a cron job on a Compute Engine instance would technically accomplish the task, but it requires provisioning and maintaining a virtual machine, which directly contradicts the requirement to avoid managing any VMs. You would also need to keep that instance running continuously or build additional automation just to start and stop it around the scheduled window. Enabling automated backups is a disaster recovery mechanism built into Cloud SQL and does not produce a targeted export of specific tables into a user-accessible bucket in a format suitable for regulatory submission. Creating a Cloud Monitoring alert is designed to respond to observed metric conditions and performance anomalies, not to execute time-based administrative workflows on a fixed schedule, making it fundamentally unsuitable for this use case.

questions.text: A financial services company runs a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL primary instance in the us-east1 region using Private IP connectivity through private service access. The team needs to create a cross-region read replica in asia-southeast1 to serve application traffic closer to their Singapore office. Both the primary and replica are configured with Private IP addresses on the same customer VPC network. After provisioning the replica, the database engineer observes that the replica cannot establish a replication connection to the primary instance. What should the engineer do to resolve this connectivity issue?

options.text: Update the VPC peering configuration to enable export and import of custom routes across the peered networks
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to update the VPC network peering so that custom routes are both exported and imported between the customer VPC and the Google-managed service producer VPCs. Cloud SQL instances connected via Private IP sit inside Google-managed VPC networks that peer with the customer VPC. When a cross-region read replica is created, the primary and replica reside in separate Google-managed VPCs, and VPC peering is non-transitive by default. This means the two Google-managed networks cannot route traffic through the customer VPC unless custom route exchange is explicitly enabled. Once you configure the peering connections to export and import custom routes, the customer VPC effectively becomes a transit network, allowing the replica in asia-southeast1 to reach the primary in us-east1 through the shared routing information.
Cloud NAT is designed to provide outbound internet access to resources without external IP addresses, so it does not facilitate internal routing between peered networks and would not solve a private connectivity issue between managed database instances. Manually creating a VPC peering connection between the two service producer networks is not possible because those networks are owned and operated by Google, and customers do not have the ability to modify their peering topology. Adding a firewall rule on the PostgreSQL port would not help because the root cause is a missing route in the network, not a blocked port. Without the proper routes being exchanged, packets from the replica simply have no path to the primary instance, regardless of what firewall rules are in place.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company runs a Cloud SQL database that powers real-time patient dashboards used by hospitals across multiple states. The company has experienced rapid and unpredictable growth in query volume, and leadership requires that the database remain accessible even if an entire Google Cloud region becomes unavailable. The team must also be able to validate their disaster recovery procedures in a safe manner that does not disrupt the live production workload. Which approach best addresses these requirements?

options.text: Deploy a cross-region failover replica and test database cloning
options.explanation:
The right approach here is to create a cross-region read replica and then test promoting that replica to confirm the disaster recovery process works as expected. A cross-region read replica maintains a continuously updated copy of the production database in a different Google Cloud region, which means the company is protected even if the primary region goes completely offline. When the team tests the promotion of this replica, they are verifying the exact recovery path they would follow in a real disaster, turning the read-only copy into a fully functional standalone primary instance. Because the original production database is not affected by the promotion test, this validation can happen without risking the live environment that hospitals depend on. This also ensures the company can meet strict RPO and RTO requirements since the replica is kept in near-real-time sync with the primary.
A zonal standby instance only protects against a zone-level failure within a single region, so it would not help if the entire region went down. Point-in-time recovery is useful when dealing with accidental data deletion or corruption, but it does not serve as a mechanism to maintain availability during a regional outage. Configuring an external replica in a separate VPC introduces significant networking complexity and does not offer the same integrated promotion workflow that a native cross-region read replica provides, making it harder to meet tight recovery time targets. Testing instance migration adds unnecessary operational overhead and does not directly validate the failover path the team would actually use during a disaster. A cross-region failover replica paired with database cloning does not verify the actual promotion and recovery workflow, since cloning creates an independent copy rather than testing the transition from replica to primary.

questions.text: A large hospital network operates a legacy PostgreSQL database that powers its electronic health records system. Due to strict data sovereignty laws, all patient data must be stored and processed on infrastructure physically located within the hospital's own data centers. The database engineering team has been asked to modernize the system using AlloyDB Omni to gain columnar engine capabilities while maintaining high availability with automatic failover across nodes. Which deployment approach should you recommend?

options.text: Run AlloyDB Omni inside a Docker container on an on-premises physical server
options.explanation:
The Kubernetes-based AlloyDB Omni deployment is the correct approach because it addresses every requirement in this scenario. AlloyDB Omni includes a Kubernetes operator that manages database instances, monitors health, and performs automatic failover when a node becomes unavailable. This orchestration layer is essential for achieving the high availability expected from a mission-critical healthcare system. Deploying within the hospital's own data center satisfies the data sovereignty requirement, and the AlloyDB columnar engine delivers the analytical performance the team is looking for alongside standard transactional workloads.
Running AlloyDB Omni in a standalone Docker container on a single physical server does not provide the automated failover capabilities needed for a production healthcare system, since there is no orchestration layer to detect failures and redirect traffic to a healthy replica. Migrating to AlloyDB on Google Cloud would move patient data off-premises, directly violating the data sovereignty laws that require all data to remain within hospital-owned infrastructure. Installing AlloyDB Omni on a single virtual machine without container orchestration similarly lacks the built-in mechanisms for automatic failover, leaving the team to build and maintain manual recovery processes that are unsuitable for a mission-critical electronic health records platform.

questions.text: A financial services company runs a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database in the europe-west1 region that powers their real-time transaction processing platform. The engineering team has been asked to develop a disaster recovery plan that ensures database operations can resume in a separate region if europe-west1 experiences a complete outage. The solution should minimize data loss and reduce the time needed to bring the database back online. Which approach should the team implement?

options.text: Promote a cross-region read replica deployed in europe-west3 to a standalone primary instance
options.explanation:
Promoting a cross-region read replica is the most effective strategy here because it maintains a near-real-time copy of the database in a geographically separate region through asynchronous replication. If the primary region suffers a total outage, this replica can be promoted to become a fully independent primary instance capable of handling both read and write traffic. Although the promotion step requires manual or automated intervention, it provides the fastest path to recovery with minimal data loss compared to other options. This pattern is widely used in disaster recovery architectures where maintaining a warm standby in a distant location is essential.
High availability with a failover replica in the same region only protects against zonal failures and would not help if the entire region went offline. Scheduled exports to Cloud Storage can serve as a backup mechanism, but restoring from exports takes significantly longer and results in more data loss since the export only captures a point-in-time snapshot rather than continuously replicated data. An external replica running on a Compute Engine instance introduces significant operational complexity and does not offer the managed failover capabilities that a Cloud SQL cross-region read replica provides, making it a less reliable and harder-to-execute option during a regional disaster scenario.

questions.text: A logistics company operates a fleet of thousands of delivery vehicles, each equipped with GPS and telemetry hardware. Every few seconds, each vehicle transmits its current location, speed, engine diagnostics, and fuel level to a central ingestion pipeline. The engineering team needs a database that can handle millions of writes per second, support efficient lookups of a single vehicle's data across specific time windows, and scale seamlessly as the fleet grows. Manual intervention to redistribute data across nodes is not acceptable. Which Google Cloud database is the best fit?

options.text: Firestore
options.explanation:
Cloud Bigtable is the right choice here because it was built to handle exactly this kind of workload. It excels at sustained, high-volume write ingestion and provides low-latency range scans, which makes it ideal when you need to pull a single vehicle's telemetry across a defined time window. The row key can be designed to combine the vehicle identifier with the timestamp, enabling efficient sequential reads within a time range. As the fleet grows and data volume increases, Bigtable scales horizontally simply by adding nodes, and it automatically rebalances data without any manual sharding or partitioning effort.
Cloud SQL is a managed relational database that primarily scales vertically, making it a poor fit when millions of writes per second are expected and the dataset will grow indefinitely. Cloud Spanner offers horizontal scalability and strong consistency, but it is optimized for transactional and relational workloads rather than sustained append-heavy write patterns with simple key-based lookups. Spanner's strengths around global transactions and SQL capabilities are unnecessary overhead in this scenario. Firestore is a document-oriented database designed around application data with flexible schemas and real-time sync capabilities, not around continuous high-throughput sequential writes from hardware sensors. While all of these services can technically store timestamped records, none of them match Bigtable's combination of write throughput, efficient range scans by key, and automatic horizontal scaling.

questions.text: A nationwide healthcare network manages multiple on-premises MySQL 8.0 databases that power patient scheduling systems and real-time appointment tracking across hundreds of clinics. Each database exceeds 4 TB in size and supports continuous operations that cannot tolerate significant downtime during migration. The team wants to transition these databases to Google Cloud while preserving the current MySQL engine and ensuring optimal performance on read-intensive queries with minimal manual effort. Which approach best accomplishes this migration?

options.text: Use the spanner-migration-tool to transform the schema and migrate to Cloud Spanner
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to leverage Database Migration Service (DMS) to move the MySQL workloads into Cloud SQL Enterprise Plus. DMS handles MySQL migrations with minimal downtime by using native replication to keep the source database and destination instance synchronized until the cutover is complete. This makes it the ideal orchestration tool when continuous availability is required. Cloud SQL Enterprise Plus is the right destination because it includes an accelerated data cache that dramatically improves read performance, which directly addresses the read-intensive query patterns described in the scenario. Since Enterprise Plus runs MySQL 8.0 natively, there is no need to refactor application code or modify the existing schema, keeping manual effort to a minimum.
AlloyDB is a PostgreSQL-compatible database, which means migrating a MySQL workload to it would require converting schemas, rewriting queries, and updating application code, all of which contradicts the goal of preserving the MySQL engine. Cloud Spanner uses a fundamentally different architecture and data model, making it unsuitable when the requirement is to maintain the existing MySQL setup with minimal changes. Datastream is a change data capture and replication service that works well when streaming data into BigQuery or other analytics destinations, but it is not designed to orchestrate a full database migration with cutover coordination. Additionally, choosing the standard Enterprise tier of Cloud SQL would miss out on the performance enhancements that Enterprise Plus provides, particularly the accelerated data cache that benefits multi-terabyte databases with heavy read traffic.

questions.text: A financial services firm runs a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance in europe-west1-b that serves its core transaction processing application. A read replica in europe-west1-c powers an internal analytics platform used by the risk management team throughout the business day. Leadership wants to ensure the analytics platform stays online even if the zone hosting the replica goes down, but they have emphasized that the solution must not substantially raise infrastructure costs. What change should be made to the current setup?

options.text: Promote the existing read replica to an independent primary instance
options.explanation:
Cloud SQL supports enabling high availability directly on a read replica, independent of the primary instance. When this is turned on, Google Cloud provisions a standby instance in a different zone within the same region. If the zone running the replica fails, an automatic failover to the standby occurs, keeping the analytics workload available. This approach provides zone-level redundancy without the significant cost increase that comes with cross-region infrastructure, making it well suited to the budget constraint described in the scenario.
Creating a read replica in a separate region would introduce cross-region egress charges and higher latency, which conflicts with the requirement to keep costs contained. Promoting the read replica to a standalone primary instance would break the replication link to the original primary, meaning the analytics platform would no longer reflect current transaction data and could create data consistency problems. Relocating the replica into the same zone as the primary instance would actually reduce resilience, because a single zonal outage would simultaneously affect both the transactional database and the analytics platform, which is the opposite of what the firm needs.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company runs a PostgreSQL-based patient data platform that must remain operational during both zone-level and region-level infrastructure failures. Their compliance team requires that the system can be restored within 40 minutes of any outage and that no more than 55 minutes of data can be lost during a disaster event. The team wants a fully managed PostgreSQL solution on Google Cloud that satisfies these resilience requirements. Which approach best meets these demands?

options.text: Provision a Cloud SQL PostgreSQL regional instance with high availability enabled and a cross-region read replica that can be promoted during a regional disaster
options.explanation:
The best approach combines regional high availability with a cross-region read replica to address both zonal and regional failure scenarios. A regional Cloud SQL instance with high availability uses synchronous replication between zones, giving automatic failover protection when a single zone goes down. The cross-region read replica adds geographic redundancy by continuously receiving data through asynchronous replication. If the entire primary region becomes unavailable, the cross-region replica can be promoted to a standalone primary instance, restoring service well within the 40-minute recovery time window. Because the replica maintains a near-real-time copy of the data, potential data loss stays comfortably within the 55-minute recovery point requirement.
Distributing read replicas across zones in one region does nothing to protect against a full regional outage, and read replicas alone lack the automatic failover mechanism needed to handle even zone-level failures efficiently. Scheduling hourly exports to Cloud Storage introduces a recovery point gap tied to the export frequency and also demands significant time to provision a new instance and import a large dataset, which would likely exceed the recovery time target. Migrating to Cloud Spanner would provide strong multi-region resilience, but it requires substantial application refactoring to accommodate a different SQL dialect and data model, making it impractical when the goal is to maintain a PostgreSQL-compatible solution with minimal disruption. Backup-dependent strategies also carry the risk of losing all transactions committed after the most recent snapshot, which conflicts with strict recovery point requirements.

questions.text: A fintech startup runs a multi-tier application on Google Kubernetes Engine where the backend services need to query an AlloyDB instance. The AlloyDB database was provisioned with Private Service Access and has no public IP assigned. The team wants to ensure that database connections are both secure and economical without adding unnecessary infrastructure overhead. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Deploy a SOCKS proxy on a dedicated Compute Engine VM to relay traffic to AlloyDB
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to run the AlloyDB Auth Proxy inside the GKE cluster, typically as a sidecar container alongside the application pods. The Auth Proxy automates IAM-based authentication and manages TLS encryption transparently, which means the development team does not need to manually handle SSL certificates or configure client-side identity verification. It takes advantage of the existing private network path between GKE and AlloyDB while layering on Google-native security controls. This makes it both secure and cost-effective because no additional networking infrastructure is required beyond what already exists. The proxy also integrates naturally with Kubernetes service accounts, simplifying access management in a containerized environment.
Connecting directly through the private IP address would technically work at the network level, but it places the burden of configuring and maintaining TLS and authentication credentials on the application team, which increases operational complexity and the risk of misconfiguration. Creating a Cloud VPN tunnel is unnecessary when both the GKE cluster and the AlloyDB instance already reside within the same Google Cloud private network, and it would add cost and management overhead with no real benefit. Running a dedicated Compute Engine instance as a SOCKS proxy introduces a single point of failure, increases monthly compute costs, and provides no security or connectivity advantage over the native Auth Proxy tooling that Google provides.

questions.text: A SaaS platform provider is migrating from multiple on-premises MySQL databases into a single consolidated Google Cloud SQL for MySQL instance. After the migration, the unified database will contain roughly 120,000 tables due to a per-tenant schema isolation strategy. Performance profiling of the existing workloads shows average utilization around 12 vCPUs and 55 GB of RAM during peak hours. Which Google Cloud SQL instance configuration should you choose to maintain operational stability and align with Google recommended guidelines?

options.text: A Cloud SQL instance with 96 vCPUs and 624 GB of RAM
options.explanation:
The right choice here is the instance with 32 vCPUs and 256 GB of RAM because Google Cloud SQL imposes specific resource requirements that depend on the number of tables in the database, not just the active CPU and memory consumption of queries. When a MySQL database exceeds 50,000 tables, the internal metadata management and data dictionary overhead demand substantially more memory and processing power to keep the instance stable. Google recommends a minimum of 32 vCPUs and at least 200 GB of memory when a database contains more than 100,000 tables. This ensures the InnoDB buffer pool and table definition cache have enough room to operate without triggering crashes or degraded performance. The 32 vCPU and 256 GB configuration meets these thresholds while being the most economical option that satisfies the requirement.
The 16 vCPU and 64 GB option looks appealing based on the reported peak utilization numbers alone, but it falls well below the resource floor that Google mandates when table counts exceed 100,000. Running 120,000 tables on that instance would likely result in extremely slow restarts, frequent out-of-memory failures during schema operations, and general instability. The 64 vCPU and 480 GB option would certainly be stable, but it provides far more capacity than the metadata overhead of 120,000 tables actually requires, leading to significant unnecessary spending. Similarly, the 96 vCPU and 624 GB option is dramatically oversized and would waste budget without delivering meaningful benefits beyond what the 32 vCPU tier already provides.

questions.text: A financial services company runs a high-traffic trading portal that relies on Memorystore for Redis to cache user portfolio data and active order states. The engineering team has been tasked with designing a disaster recovery strategy that addresses a complete regional outage. In the event of such a failure, traffic will be redirected to a secondary region where a new Memorystore instance must be populated with the latest available cache data from the primary site. What approach should the team implement to ensure the standby region can restore the cache with the most current data?

options.text: Provision a Standard Tier instance with multiple read replicas
options.explanation:
The correct approach here is to export Redis data into a Cloud Storage bucket that spans two or more regions. When using a dual-region or multi-region bucket, the exported RDB file is replicated across geographic boundaries, meaning it stays available even if the primary region becomes completely unreachable. During a failover event, the operations team can spin up a new Memorystore instance in the secondary region and import this file, restoring cached portfolio and order state data without starting from scratch.
Configuring RDB snapshots at a high frequency captures point-in-time backups, but those snapshots are stored within the service backend of the same region and cannot be directly accessed or imported by an instance in a different region. This makes them unsuitable as the sole mechanism for cross-region disaster recovery. Enabling High Availability on a Redis cluster provides automatic failover between zones within the same region, which protects against zonal failures but does nothing to address a scenario where the entire region is offline. Provisioning a Standard Tier instance with multiple read replicas similarly improves read throughput and provides intra-region redundancy across zones, but replicas are confined to the same region as the primary node and would be lost alongside it during a full regional outage.

questions.text: A global insurance company is planning to migrate its claims processing database from on-premises infrastructure to Google Cloud. The application makes extensive use of PostgreSQL-specific features including custom extensions, stored procedures, and advanced indexing. The company requires automatic failover with a Recovery Time Objective under 10 minutes and near-zero data loss in the event of a zonal outage. Due to regulatory obligations, all transactional writes must remain within a single designated country. However, claims adjusters and compliance teams across multiple continents need fast read access to the data without noticeable delays. Which database architecture best meets these requirements?

options.text: A Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance with high availability enabled and regional persistent disk storage
options.explanation:
The best architecture here is AlloyDB with high availability and cross-region read replicas. AlloyDB is fully compatible with PostgreSQL, which means the company can migrate its stored procedures, custom extensions, and advanced indexing without a rewrite. The high-availability configuration uses a disaggregated storage layer that separates compute from data, enabling rapid automated failover that comfortably meets the sub-10-minute RTO requirement while also minimizing data loss. Cross-region read replicas allow claims adjusters and compliance teams on other continents to query the data with low latency, while all write operations remain confined to the designated regulatory region.
Cloud SQL with high availability and regional storage provides solid zonal resilience within a single region, but it lacks native cross-region read replicas that would give low-latency access to users on different continents. Cloud Spanner distributed across multiple continents would handle global reads well, but it uses a different SQL dialect and data model that would require significant reworking of the existing PostgreSQL stored procedures and extensions. The option describing multi-region Cloud SQL with internal load balancing to distribute writes globally directly contradicts the regulatory mandate that all transactional writes must stay within one specific country, making it an invalid choice.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company is building a data processing pipeline using multiple service projects connected through a Shared VPC hosted in a central network project. A database engineer needs to provision a new Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance in one of the service projects with connectivity restricted to internal IP addresses only. During the provisioning attempt, the engineer encounters a "Network association failed" error when trying to assign a private IP address to the instance using the host project's VPC network. The engineer must resolve this error while keeping all database traffic off the public internet. What should the engineer do to fix this issue?

options.text: Enable the Service Networking API in the host project where the VPC resides
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to configure Private Services Access on the Shared VPC network prior to creating the Cloud SQL instance. Private Services Access involves reserving an IP address range within your VPC and then creating a private connection between your VPC network and the Google-managed services network where Cloud SQL instances actually run. This private connection is implemented as a VPC Network Peering link that allows the Cloud SQL control plane to allocate an internal IP address from your reserved range and attach the instance to your network. Without completing this setup first, the system has no mechanism to bridge your VPC and the managed services network, which directly causes the "Network association failed" error.
Enabling the Service Networking API is a prerequisite that must be done before you can use Private Services Access, but simply enabling the API does not create the reserved IP range or the private connection itself. The API activation alone will not resolve the network association failure. Manually creating a VPC Network Peering connection to the Google-managed tenant project is not feasible because you do not have access to or control over the Google-managed project where Cloud SQL resources are hosted. Switching to a public IP address with authorized networks would bypass the association error, but it violates the stated requirement that all database traffic must remain off the public internet and defeats the purpose of using a Shared VPC with private connectivity.

questions.text: A healthcare technology company uses Google Cloud Spanner to manage a catalog of medical research articles. They want to build a feature that allows researchers to discover articles with conceptually related findings, even when those articles use different terminology or phrasing. The feature must deliver low-latency results and avoid introducing additional infrastructure or data synchronization overhead. Which approach should the company take to meet these requirements?

options.text: Export the Spanner data to a Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance and leverage the pgvector extension to run similarity queries against article content
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to use Spanner's native vector search capability, which allows embeddings to be stored directly alongside relational data and queried using the VECTOR_SEARCH function. Embeddings are numerical representations of text that capture semantic meaning, so two articles about the same concept will have similar vectors even if they use completely different words. The Euclidean distance metric measures the spatial proximity between two vectors, making it well suited to ranking results by conceptual similarity. Because this functionality is built into Spanner, there is no need to move data to another system or maintain synchronization between databases. This keeps the architecture simple and ensures the low-latency performance that Spanner is designed to deliver at scale.
Full Text Search indexes and the SEARCH function are optimized for keyword-based retrieval and linguistic stemming, which means they match on specific terms rather than understanding the deeper semantic relationship between different phrases. A secondary index combined with LIKE queries and wildcards is limited to pattern matching on character sequences and cannot interpret whether two differently worded passages convey similar ideas. Exporting data to a Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance with pgvector would introduce unnecessary complexity, requiring a separate database to manage along with a pipeline to keep the data in sync, which directly conflicts with the requirement to avoid additional infrastructure and synchronization overhead. Each of these alternatives either lacks the ability to perform true semantic similarity search or adds architectural burden that the native Spanner solution eliminates.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company stores patient monitoring data in a Bigtable instance that powers a real-time alerting system. The alerting system primarily queries data from the current week, but compliance policies require the company to retain all patient records for a minimum of seven years. The storage consumption in the Bigtable cluster has been growing rapidly, and the operations team has been asked to reduce costs while ensuring that historical data remains accessible if needed during an audit. What is the best strategy to address this requirement?

options.text: Export data older than six months to Cloud Storage and remove it from Bigtable
options.explanation:
The most appropriate approach is to offload aged data into Cloud Storage and then delete it from the Bigtable instance. Cloud Storage offers significantly lower per-gigabyte pricing compared to Bigtable SSD or HDD storage, making it ideal when the data is rarely accessed but must be retained to satisfy compliance obligations. Because the alerting system only needs the current week of data, keeping years of historical records in Bigtable is unnecessarily expensive. A scheduled Dataflow pipeline can handle the export and deletion of old rows, which is a well-established pattern in large-scale NoSQL environments. This keeps the Bigtable cluster lean and focused on the active workload while still preserving historical records in an affordable storage tier.
Increasing the node count does not solve a storage cost problem because Bigtable nodes primarily govern processing throughput, and storage is billed independently based on the total gigabytes stored regardless of the number of nodes. Enabling GZIP compression manually is not a meaningful action because Bigtable already applies automatic compression at the storage layer, so there is no user-configurable compression setting that would yield additional savings. Moving data to a separate Bigtable instance still incurs the same high storage costs per gigabyte since it is the same underlying storage technology, and it adds operational complexity without delivering the cost reduction that an archival-oriented service provides.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics startup deploys its data processing application on Google Kubernetes Engine. The application connects to a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database that stores sensitive patient metrics. During a recent compliance review, auditors flagged that the database credentials have not been changed in over six months. Management now requires that the database password be automatically refreshed every 60 days and that the running application always has access to the latest valid credentials without any downtime. You need to design a solution that meets these requirements. What should you do?

options.text: Store the database credentials in Secret Manager and configure a 60-day rotation schedule using a Cloud Function
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to leverage Secret Manager with an automated rotation workflow driven by a Cloud Function. Secret Manager provides a centralized, auditable location to store sensitive credentials, and the rotation schedule ensures a Cloud Function fires every 60 days to update both the password in the Cloud SQL instance and the corresponding secret version. The application retrieves the latest credential through a straightforward API call, which means there is no downtime and no need to redeploy containers or manually coordinate password changes across the cluster.
Encrypting the password with Cloud KMS and baking it into a container image creates a tightly coupled dependency between the credential lifecycle and the container build and deployment process. Every time the password changes, you would need to rebuild and redeploy the image across all nodes, making a 60-day automated rotation impractical. Storing credentials in a Cloud Storage configuration file lacks any built-in mechanism to trigger and coordinate password rotation on a schedule, leaving the process manual and error-prone even if access is restricted through IAM. IAM database authentication uses short-lived tokens rather than traditional passwords, so it would not fulfill an explicit compliance requirement that specifically mandates periodic rotation of a database password. Additionally, that option describes mapping individual analysts to separate database roles, which does not align with how a single application service account typically authenticates to a shared backend database.

options.text: Encrypt the database password with a Cloud KMS key and embed the resulting ciphertext directly into the application's container image
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to leverage Secret Manager with an automated rotation workflow driven by a Cloud Function.
